rationale_for_baseline_tier: A mild articular/constitutional SLE flare with no serositis, nephritis or septic-joint features can be managed by restarting hydroxychloroquine, NSAIDs and rest, with urgent GP/rheumatology review — a T2 baseline. Because she lives alone with a severe flare, the threshold for review is low, and organ-crisis/septic-joint safety-nets are mandatory.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Sharp chest pain on breathing, breathlessness, or palpitations
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you get sharp chest pain on breathing, become breathless, or get palpitations, go to the emergency department.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Cola/red-coloured urine, or a single joint that becomes bright red, hot and agony to move with a fever
escalation_edges.rationale: Suggests lupus nephritis or septic arthritis.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If your urine turns cola/red-coloured, or a single joint becomes bright red, hot and agony to move with a fever, go to the emergency department.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Lives alone with a severe flare
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Lower the threshold for review; arrange a support person overnight.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: This is a joint-and-fatigue lupus flare, most likely from missing your hydroxychloroquine — your screening is reassuring so your organs aren't being damaged tonight. Restart your hydroxychloroquine at the usual dose, use an anti-inflammatory with food and warm packs, rest and hydrate, and book urgent GP/rheumatology review in a couple of days. Because you're on your own, ask your sister to check in, and go to ED for chest pain on breathing, cola/red urine, a single hot red joint with fever, or a high fever.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Urgent GP/rheumatology within 48-72 h; ED for organ/septic-joint red flags
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: Attend ED overnight if any organ or septic-joint red flag develops.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Telehealth review of a mild articular/constitutional SLE flare (likely from missed hydroxychloroquine); serositis, nephritis and septic arthritis screened negative; hydroxychloroquine restarted, NSAIDs/rest advised; organ-crisis/septic-joint safety-net given; urgent rheumatology review recommended.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Dismissing without an organ/septic-joint screen or safety-net.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: An evolving serositis/pericardial tamponade, lupus nephritis, or a septic joint is missed and progresses.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Routine review is acceptable only with an organ/septic-joint safety-net and low threshold given she lives alone.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: ED/ambulance for an organ-negative articular flare is over-triage.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Cannot examine the chest/heart, test urine or obtain bloods remotely to detect subclinical organ involvement.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Screen organ/septic-joint red flags by history, restart hydroxychloroquine + NSAIDs, and give a safety-net with a low threshold for review.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Either over-triage to ED or reassure without an organ/septic-joint screen.
